dependencies.notes: Redis itself has no dependencies. But it is a dependency for auth-service (sessions, rate limits, lockout state) and inventory-service (stock cache). A Redis outage silently degrades auth — auth falls back to DB but at much higher latency. Monitor auth p99 latency as a signal of Redis health.

common_issues.title: Redis OOM — out of memory / writes failing
symptoms: auth-service logs: 'OOM command not allowed when used memory > maxmemory'
New session creation failing while reads still work
Redis INFO memory shows used_memory > maxmemory
likely_causes: Eviction policy set to noeviction — writes blocked when memory full
Memory limit too low for current session volume
Key expiry not being set on some session types — keys accumulating indefinitely
Memory fragmentation causing effective capacity to be lower than configured

steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli info memory | grep -E 'used_memory_human|maxmemory_human|mem_fragmentation_ratio|maxmemory_policy'
steps.note: Key values: used_memory near maxmemory = near limit. mem_fragmentation_ratio > 1.5 = significant fragmentation. maxmemory_policy = noeviction is dangerous.

steps.action: Immediately change eviction policy to allow writes
steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
steps.note: This is a live config change — takes effect immediately. allkeys-lru will evict the least recently used keys when memory is full. This may evict some sessions (users get logged out) but is better than complete write failure. This change does not persist across restarts — update the configmap too.

steps.action: Make the eviction policy change persistent
steps.note: Find the maxmemory-policy line and change to allkeys-lru. If you can't find the configmap, ask Carol. The in-memory change from step 2 will be lost on pod restart.

steps.action: Increase maxmemory if eviction is too aggressive
steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory 8gb
steps.note: Current limit is 4GB. Increasing to 8GB is the quick fix. The node must have available memory — check with 'kubectl top node'. Make this persistent in the configmap. File a capacity ticket.

steps.action: Check for keys without TTL (potential leak)
steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli --scan --pattern 'session:*' | head -20 | xargs -I{} redis-cli TTL {}
steps.note: TTL of -1 means no expiry — these keys accumulate forever. This is likely a bug in auth-service. Keys should have expiry matching JWT lifetime. Report to identity team.

common_issues.escalation: Redis OOM causing auth write failures is P1 — sessions cannot be created. Page platform on-call. Notify identity team.

common_issues.title: Counter reset after Redis failover / restart
symptoms: Duplicate invoice numbers or IDs after a Redis restart
Rate limit counters reset — users who were throttled can suddenly make requests again
Account lockout counters cleared — brute force protection temporarily bypassed
likely_causes: Counters stored in Redis with no persistence or backup — Redis treats them as ephemeral
Redis AOF or RDB persistence not enabled or not recovering correctly
Failover promoted a replica that was slightly behind

steps.note: A suspiciously low DBSIZE after a restart/failover confirms the data was lost. Compare against what it was before — check Datadog Redis metrics for 'connected_clients' and 'keyspace_hits'.

steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG GET save && redis-cli CONFIG GET appendonly
steps.note: appendonly should be 'yes'. save should have values like '3600 1 300 100 60 10000'. If appendonly is 'no' and save is empty, Redis is fully in-memory — no persistence at all. That's a configuration error.

steps.action: For invoice number specifically — migrate to UUID or DB-sequence
steps.command: N/A — this is a code change in payments-service.
steps.note: Redis counters should not be used for anything that requires uniqueness guarantees across restarts. Invoice numbers in Redis is an architectural error. This was fixed in payments-service v3.18.x — verify that version is deployed.

steps.action: For rate limit / lockout counters — accept the reset and monitor
steps.command: Monitor auth logs for brute force attempts immediately after a Redis restart.
steps.note: Not much you can do about lost rate limit state in real-time. The main risk is that a brute force attacker who was locked out gets a new window of attempts. Monitor and re-block if needed.

common_issues.escalation: If security-sensitive counters (lockout, rate limit) were reset during an active attack, treat as security incident. Page security team.

common_issues.title: Redis cluster node down / split-brain
symptoms: Some keys inaccessible (wrong slot / redirected to dead node)
Replication lag visible in redis-cli cluster info
likely_causes: Node pod crashed or was evicted by Kubernetes
Node ran out of memory and was OOMKilled by kernel

steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster info | grep cluster_state
steps.note: cluster_state:fail means the cluster has lost quorum. You need to investigate which nodes are down.

steps.action: List cluster nodes and their states
steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster nodes
steps.note: Look for nodes with 'fail' or 'disconnected' flag. Note their IDs.

steps.action: Check if failed node pods are running in Kubernetes
steps.note: If a Redis pod is in CrashLoopBackOff or Pending, that's your failed node. Fix the pod first, then let the cluster auto-recover.

steps.action: If node is running but cluster thinks it's down, try manual failover
steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster reset soft
steps.note: Try soft reset first. If that doesn't work, you're in deeper trouble than this runbook covers. Get Carol on the call.

steps.action: After recovery, verify all shards have quorum
steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster info | grep -E 'cluster_size|cluster_known_nodes|cluster_slots_ok'
steps.note: cluster_slots_ok should equal 16384 (total slots). Any missing slots = data loss risk.

common_issues.escalation: Redis cluster split-brain is P1. Page platform on-call immediately. Do not attempt to force-write to a degraded cluster.

common_issues.title: High Redis latency / slow commands
symptoms: auth-service p99 latency elevated even when auth pods are healthy
likely_causes: Large key scan operation blocking the single-threaded event loop
High memory fragmentation causing GC-like pauses
KEYS * command being run by someone — never do this in production
Network latency between auth-service and Redis (cross-AZ traffic)

steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli SLOWLOG GET 10
steps.note: Shows recent slow commands with execution time in microseconds. KEYS *, SMEMBERS on large sets, or SORT commands are usual suspects.

steps.action: Check if KEYS * is being used anywhere
steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli MONITOR | head -100
steps.note: MONITOR is extremely high overhead — only run for a few seconds. KEYS * in production is forbidden — it blocks all other operations. If you see it, find who's running it and stop them immediately.

steps.command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli info memory | grep mem_fragmentation_ratio
steps.note: Ratio > 1.5 is concerning, > 2.0 is bad. You can trigger a defrag: redis-cli CONFIG SET activedefrag yes. This runs in the background and reduces fragmentation over time.

steps.action: Check cross-AZ traffic if latency is consistent
steps.command: Check whether auth-service pods are in the same AZ as the Redis primary. kubectl get pods -l app=auth-service -o wide (check node). kubectl get pods -l app=redis-0 -o wide (check node).
steps.note: Cross-AZ Redis calls add 1-3ms per call. With many calls per request, this adds up. Ideally, auth-service and Redis primary should be in the same AZ. Topology-aware routing is a future improvement — JIRA PLAT-189.

common_issues.escalation: Redis latency affecting auth is P2. Notify #platform-eng. If p99 auth latency exceeds 1 second, escalate to P1.

general_health_checks: kubectl exec redis-0 -- redis-cli ping — should return PONG
kubectl exec redis-0 -- redis-cli info memory | grep used_memory_human
kubectl exec redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster info | grep cluster_state — expect 'ok'
Check Datadog: 'Redis Overview' — ops/sec, connected clients, memory usage, hit rate
kubectl exec redis-0 -- redis-cli info stats | grep evicted_keys — non-zero means eviction is happening
known_gaps: Redis persistence config is in a configmap but live CONFIG SET changes don't persist through pod restarts — the two can drift
No automated test for Redis failover behavior in auth-service
Cross-AZ topology-aware routing not implemented — JIRA PLAT-189
KEYS * is not blocked at the ACL level — relies on developer discipline
Backup verification procedure not documented — we assume RDB backups work but haven't tested restore recently
notes: Redis is shared infrastructure. Changes to Redis config affect all consumers (auth, inventory, gateway). Coordinate any planned Redis maintenance with all consuming teams in advance. Give at least 24 hours notice for planned restarts.
